subject: ap-csp
theme: All digital data is represented in binary, and understanding how that representation introduces limits — overflow, round-off errors, compression tradeoffs — is essential for responsible data analysis.
core_concepts: Computers store all data as bits (0s and 1s). N bits can represent 2^N distinct values — for example, 8 bits represent 256 values (0–255). To represent N distinct items, you need enough bits so that 2^bits is greater than or equal to N.

Analog data (sound waves, temperature) changes continuously; digital data uses discrete values. Sampling converts analog signals to digital by measuring the signal at regular intervals — higher sampling rates produce higher quality digital representations.

Overflow errors occur when a computed number exceeds the maximum value a fixed number of bits can hold. Round-off errors occur because many decimal values (like 0.1) cannot be represented exactly in binary floating-point format.

Lossless compression reduces file size while allowing perfect reconstruction of the original data — used for text, ZIP files, and PNG images. Lossy compression (JPEG, MP3) achieves greater reduction by permanently discarding some data that is less perceptible to humans.

Metadata is data that describes other data — such as a photo's GPS location, creation date, file size, and camera model. Metadata often reveals sensitive information about users even when the content itself seems private.

Big data refers to datasets so large and fast-changing that traditional analysis tools are insufficient. Analyzing big data can reveal useful patterns, but it also introduces significant privacy risks through data aggregation.

Data bias occurs when data systematically under- or over-represents part of the population. Biased data produces biased models — correlation in data should not be confused with causation, and conclusions drawn from filtered or incomplete data may not generalize.
exam_tip: The AP exam tests bit-counting (how many bits do you need for N values — use 2^N) and compression tradeoffs — know that lossless preserves all data and is required for precision tasks (medical imaging), while lossy is preferred when file size matters more than perfection (streaming audio/video).
